data.title: Diversifying Dividends: Sketching Out My Battle Plan For 2026
data.description:
"No plan survives first contact with the enemy." (Unknown)

"Plans are worthless, but planning is everything." (Dwight Eisenhower)

These two quotes encapsulate my thoughts about forming an investment plan for next year.

How can one plan out what to invest in if they don't know what economic circumstances will be? Whether the economy slides toward recession or stages a robust rebound? What stock market prices or valuations will be? Whether some black swan rears its head? Whether something happens to prove AI either overhyped or even more transformative than the market thought? What the president or Congress do?

Plans are worthless in a world of so many moving pieces.

And yet, planning is indispensable.

For an active investor, I think this means that one should always have not only financial goals but a general investment strategy to achieve those goals. However, the specific tactics may shift over time as the market or economy changes or as the investor's knowledge and awareness of opportunities grows.

As I begin to think about 2026, I'm sketching out the outline of a plan.

About 45% of my taxable portfolio is in REITs. I'll discuss below why I think it has made sense to concentrate that heavily in the real estate sector in recent years, but at this point, I want to diversify my portfolio. The easiest and smartest (for me) way to do that is by increasing my allocation to ETFs.

So here's where I'm going in this article:

  • A broad snapshot of the US economy and stock market valuation.
  • A defense of my portfolio concentration in REITs but also why and how I intend to diversify my dividend growth portfolio in 2026.
  • Some comments on my current buy list.

Onward!

Market Situation Report

Here's the "TL/DR" version:

  • The US economy is remarkably steady and resilient but by no means going gangbusters, because strength in certain areas is offset by weakness elsewhere.
  • The stock market is expensive and will likely generate poor returns over the next 5-10 years, but current earnings momentum could continue to support strong index performance in the short run.

Now here's the slightly longer version.

The economy was mediocre, steady, and K-shaped for most of 2024, and that steady mediocrity has continued into 2025. Economic forecasts for 2026 growth began to pick up in the latter months of 2024, perhaps driven by rising odds of regime change in Washington DC and an extension of the 2017 tax cuts. That rising optimism crashed after President Trump initiated a multi-front trade war, putting up tariffs on virtually every country in the world (and even some islands inhabited only by penguins!). Thereafter, whether it was all according to plan or "TACO," trade deals began to be implemented, and growth projections began rising again.

At this point, US real GDP growth is widely expected to fall somewhere in the neighborhood of 1.5% to 2% in 2026.

Of course, it's always possible that some shock or another hits us and we do slip into recession.

But short of some shock, it doesn't seem likely that corporations are going to stop or greatly slow down data center construction or that affluent Americans will stop or greatly slow down spending.

It is remarkable how steady US economic growth has been the last few years. Take a look at The Conference Board's coincident indicator (various metrics that give a real-time view of the economy) growth in the post-COVID period:

From the 1950s through the 2010s, it has mostly been a story of wild swings up and down. But over the last 2-3 years, it has been remarkably rangebound at a touch below 2% annual growth.

My explanation for this phenomenon would be that in the 20th century, the US economy was overwhelmingly driven by cyclical sectors, including manufacturing. But in the 21st century, the US has become increasingly concentrated in technology and services, which just aren't very cyclical.

I don't envision the US returning to the olden days (the halcyon days, according to some) of a manufacturing driven economy. Therefore, the economy just isn't as cyclical as it used to be. Recessions aren't as common as they used to be. And the shocks that put us into recession now have to be bigger.

That economic steadiness may have played a role in the rising degree of complacency that characterizes the stock market.

Of course, the bigger factor driving stock prices and valuations higher has been optimism about artificial intelligence. You don't see the S&P 500 (SPY) zoom above a 20x forward price/earnings ratio unless there's a lot of investor optimism in the air.

AI is an amazing technology, and it holds lots of promise to increase productivity and fuel faster economic growth.

But like railroads, electricity, radio, the Internet, or (arguably) cryptocurrency, the initial implementation and growth of AI coincides with rapid increase in related asset prices.

As the erudite Howard Marks explained in a recent note titled "Is It a Bubble?", we can't say for sure that AI is a bubble or that its associated infrastructure is being overbuilt, but it would be extremely rare if such an amazing new technology came about without a coinciding bubble.

In any case, when we look at past instances in which the market sported the same valuation it currently does, we find that forward returns multiple years out have historically been flat on average.

Over the next year after reaching our current valuation, just about anything could happen. Price movement could be flat, or it crash by double-digits, or it could go up by double-digits.

I have no idea what the S&P 500 is going to do in 2026, and I don't think anyone else does either. But as you'll notice in the chart above on the right, there have been no historical instances in which the index has generated total returns above the low single-digits after rising above a 22x forward P/E ratio.

This time could be different. Of course it could.

But if you're a betting person, wouldn't it be more rational to bet that history will repeat this time?

Zooming out to 10-year forward returns at (or near) our current valuation, we again find extremely few instances of positive total returns:

I usually prefer simply looking at the fundamentals rather than relying on statistics alone.

But if you find there to be too many nuances, too many complications, too many moving pieces within the fundamentals, wouldn't it be reasonable to fall back on the historical statistics?

Portfolio SitRep

I have 45% of my portfolio in REITs right now. There are a few reasons why I believe this concentration has made sense for me.

First, my work background is in real estate, initially on the residential side and then the commercial side. I understand this industry better than any other. My investing experience has convinced me that I should only own individual stocks when I have deep knowledge of the company, including its business model, balance sheet, and management.

If you imagine two concentric circles in which the outer circle is one's "sphere of competence" and the inner circle is one's "sphere of proficiency," my preference is to own stocks that fall within that inner circle of true proficiency.

That's where the investor has the biggest competitive advantage in a market dominated by generalists.

Second, over the last 2-3 years, REITs have endured a brutal bear market as a result of rising interest rates and a massive wave of new supply, especially for the multifamily, industrial, and life science sub-sectors.

Those headwinds have dragged REITs as a whole down to their cheapest level relative to the broader market since the heights of the Dot Com bubble in 2000.

So, publicly listed CRE sports a low valuation. But what about its fundamentals?

Well, generally speaking, the sector has suffered a major slowdown in growth over the last few years as REITs absorb higher interest rates and a huge amount of new supply.

The good news is that both of those headwinds are fading.

The Fed just lowered their key policy rate again, and current expectations are for another 1-3 cuts next year.

Historically, Fed rate-cutting cycles have typically coincided with declines in long-term interest rates.

That's good news for REITs, which are among the most rate-sensitive stocks on the market.

The other fading headwind is the level of new supply (new buildings and/or leasable space) coming online.

In 2021-2022, when interest rates were ultra-low and the stimulus-driven economy was red hot, there was huge ramp-up in development starts. But as often happens during periods of fast growth and high optimism, developers overshot future demand. As such, from the second half of 2023 through 2024, an excessive amount of real estate came to market, raising vacancy rates and increasing competition for tenants.

At this point, however, the development pipeline has significantly shrunk. On an inflation-adjusted basis, CRE construction spending is lower today than it was before the pandemic began, and it is expected to drop further in the coming quarters.

This should coincide with a modest rebound in demand, as long as the economy holds up in the coming years.

With valuation low and fundamentals improving, it is plausible that we see a similar spree of outperformance over the market as what we saw after the Dot Com bubble. Compare the performance of the Vanguard Real Estate Fund (VGSIX) to the Nasdaq Index (QQQ) both during and in the years after the Internet bubble:

Real estate couldn't catch a bid during the bubble, when investors cared only about finding the next hot tech name. But when the feverish tech enthusiasm faded, the steady cash-compounding of real estate slowly became appreciated again.

Now, I would never predict this level of outperformance over the next five years. Many things are different about today's stock market, economy, and demographics than those of the late 1990s.

But enough things are similar that meaningful outperformance of REITs as a whole seems plausible.

That said, 45% of my portfolio is a pretty significant concentration.

Over time, I want to slowly diversify my portfolio and reduce that heavy concentration in REITs.

But how do I do that when, as I said above, I only want to invest in individual stocks that are within my sphere of proficiency, or at least my sphere of competence.

Part of the solution is to expand my sphere of competence by continuing to learn more about other sectors, industries, and business models. Much of the joy of investing is in the opportunity to continuously learn and expand one's mental horizons.

But the primary solution, for me, is to lean more heavily on diversified dividend ETFs.

Here are four ETFs with little to no exposure to real estate that I plan to begin systematically buying every month next year:

Top 2 Sectors Dividend Yield Projected Dividend Growth Rate (Guesstimate) Expense Ratio
VictoryShares US Lg Cap High Div Vol-Weighted ETF (CDL) Utilities & Consumer Defensive 3.3% Mid- to High-Single-Digit 0.35%
iShares Core Dividend Growth ETF (DGRO) Financials & Technology 2.1% High-Single-Digit 0.08%
First Trust Morningstar Dividend Leaders ETF (FDL) Energy & Healthcare 3.8% Mid- to High-Single-Digit 0.43%
iShares Ultra Short Duration Bond Active ETF (ICSH) N/A ~3.75% N/A 0.08%

Obviously, ICSH is a cash equivalent vehicle whose primary role in my portfolio is as a stabilizer and dry powder. For more on that topic, see my article, "ICSH: A Love Letter To Cash."

If broader market valuations were lower, then I probably wouldn't allocate significantly to ICSH. But with valuations elevated, I do want to keep a cash position somewhere in the neighborhood of 2-5% of my total portfolio value.

CDL is an ETF that I've talked about multiple times in recent articles. I like its defensiveness via the large utilities and staples exposure as well as the enhanced growth prospects of utilities from growth in demand for electricity.

DGRO is my favorite alternative to the S&P 500 for dividend growth investors, as I explored in this article. It has 400 holdings, a distinctly value and quality tilt compared to the S&P 500, and has generated better long-term dividend growth than its passive ETF peers.

Lastly, despite its higher expense ratio, I view FDL as a good alternative to the Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD). I explained in this article why SCHD has underperformed recently, and FDL's different stock-picking methodology has prevented it from the unfortunate reconstitution issues that have held back SCHD recently. FDL basically screens for well-covered dividends and then holds a 100-stock basket of the highest yielding ones.

My plan is to systematically buy these four ETFs every month, allocating over half of my investable cash (aside from what's specifically set aside for dry powder) to them.

Of course, if some individual stock becomes irresistible, I'm not going to refrain from buying it. But the overall goal is to diversify my portfolio via ETFs in 2026.

My Current Dividend Buy List

So, remember a few seconds ago when I said I want to reduce my portfolio allocation to REITs?

That's next year's goal. In what remains of 2025, I want to top up my positions in a handful of attractively valued REITs in order to fulfill my obsessive-compulsive need for a round number of shares.

Dividend Yield Projected Dividend Growth Rate (Guesstimate)
Agree Realty (ADC) 4.4% Mid-Single-Digit
American Healthcare REIT (AHR) 2.1% Low-Double-Digit
American Homes 4 Rent (AMH) 3.9% Mid-Single-Digit
American Tower (AMT) 3.8% Mid-Single-Digit

Wow, what a patriotic list. Lots of America!

It also just so happens that all four stocks start with "A." That wasn't intentional.

Agree Realty

ADC is my largest holding and an ideal anchor position for a dividend growth portfolio, in my view. It is defensive and recession-resistant with a conservatively managed balance sheet, but it is also capable of consistent, mid-single-digit growth via its multiple channels of growth. A 4.4% yield plus 4-5% annual growth plus some valuation upside brings total return prospects to the high-single-digits or into the double-digits.

American Healthcare REIT

AHR is a senior-focused REIT that primarily owns multi-service / continuum-of-care campuses, senior housing operating properties, and some outpatient medical buildings.

The REIT also boasts quite a strong balance sheet, with net debt to EBITDA of 3.5x and a weighted average debt maturity of about 12.5 years.

Healthcare REITs, including those focused on senior housing like AHR, are dipping right now in anticipation of lost Affordable Care Act subsidies in 2026. While this loss should have little direct impact on senior housing and skilled nursing facilities, it could have a significant indirect impact by causing a hit to state budgets, which could then lead to cuts in reimbursements to Medicaid recipients such as senior care operators.

That's my rudimentary understanding anyway.

To be honest, the size of impact this could have is well outside my sphere of competence. But I do find it reasonable to assume the negative impact will be small and/or brief to senior-focused REITs like AHR.

The long run outlook for owners of this type of real estate still looks incredibly strong.

After years of overbuilding during the 2010s in anticipation of the coming demographic wave, construction of senior housing is now around its lowest point in decades while the population of 80+ seniors is growing rapidly. That should create a growing gap between supply and demand into the future, resulting in a widening shortage:

Analysts forecast double-digit earnings growth for at least the next few years, and I think it's likely that streak of double-digit growth continues thereafter.

Hence why I'm buying it even at a dividend yield of 2.2% and a price/FFO multiple of 28x. (Compare that to Welltower's (WELL) 35x FFO multiple and 1.6% yield.)

I pitched AHR as a good buy in June in my article "American Healthcare REIT's Growth Story Is Still Young," and the REIT has doubled the S&P 500's returns since then. I don't know if AHR will produce that level of performance in 2026, but I do like its long-term growth prospects a lot.

American Homes 4 Rent

AMH has been a melting ice cube in the face of a temporarily oversupplied residential rental market and low job growth.

But the good news for AMH is that:

  1. Homebuying affordability is very low. Renting a single-family home is significantly cheaper than buying one. It would take something heroic to bring buying costs back into parity with renting costs.
  2. Millennials are in the prime single-family years of life, which should ensure robust renter demand going forward.
  3. The amount of new single-family and multifamily housing under construction is now quite low and continuing to decline.
  4. AMH's portfolio concentration in the more affordable Sunbelt and Midwest regions position it well to benefit from domestic migration trends.
  5. AMH's internal development platform allow it to continue growing (and increasing the quality of) its SFR portfolio when it ceases to be profitable to acquire properties in the existing home market.

The following are some charts to illustrate the points above.

First, this chart shows that holding home prices equal, mortgage rates would need to drop to 3.7% to make homebuying as affordable as renting:

Next, this chart from a Federal Reserve survey shows that consistently 70% of renters prefer to own their home but that from 2019 to 2025, there has been a material step down in the share of renters who believe they will buy a home in the future.

Next, this chart shows that the amount of single-family and multifamily housing under construction is falling:

Finally, as a result of all points above as well as a steady economy and labor market, SFR rent growth appears to have stabilized:

Whether SFR rent growth will rebound, decline, or remain rangebound depends on the trajectory of the broader economy going forward.

At an almost 4% yield, steady mid-single-digit growth, and a valuation below 18x AFFO (close to its lowest level in its existence as a public company), AMH is compelling as a long-term dividend growth investment.

American Tower

Lastly, I'll keep my comments brief on AMT because I don't have anything new to add from last week's article. The long-term prospects for AMT remain attractive, as mobile data usage is almost certain to keep growing at a rapid pace deep into the future.

While leasing and churn can create volatility from year to year, towers and colocation data centers will continue to play an integral role in the economy for decades to come, and AMT looks well-positioned to compound cash flows because of that.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: JIVE: This Hidden Gem ETF Is A Superb Blend Of Diversification, Growth, And Alpha
data.description:
Introduction

With the plethora of ETFs hitting the market in recent years, I've grown accustomed to owning them in my portfolio for diversification. However, thanks to two readers, I wasn't aware of this relatively new ETF until last week.

But after further research, it became apparent that this ETF could be a superstar in the making.

Moreover, funds with non-U.S. exposure like JPMorgan International Value ETF (JIVE) could be poised to outperform if economists are correct with their projections.

Still relatively new, JIVE has performed well, up nearly 36% in the past year alone, significantly outperforming some of its peers and popular U.S.-based ETFs.

If you're someone currently searching for an ETF that offers diversification, income, and outperformance potential, then consider this hidden gem for your portfolio.

Why International Diversification?

If you're a U.S.-based investor, then you may be wondering, what's the need to diversify outside of U.S. based equities? For centuries the United States has been the world's dominant power. But in recent years the U.S. dollar has been on a rapid decline.

According to Charles Schwab (SCHW), international stocks could be poised to see strong growth in the years ahead.

Economies like India, Philippines, and Saudi Arabia are expected to experience strong growth for 2025. Combined, they are projected to lead the U.S., often considered the dominator when it comes to GDP growth.

This growth is led by population growth, rising consumer demand, and the adoption of artificial intelligence.

Moreover, for years non-U.S. equities were significantly undervalued in comparison to US stocks. So, tapping into this growth potential may be another reason for diversifying outside of U.S. equities.

Why JIVE Stands Out

JPMorgan International Value ETF is an actively managed fund launched by asset manager JPMorgan (JPM).

Although the fund is actively managed, which I like as this could lead to outperformance, their management fee is reasonable at 0.55%.

Meaning for every $10,000 invested, you would pay $55 annually. While this is significantly higher than one of my favorite international ETFs like the Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF (SCHY), JIVE's actively managed portfolio gives it a higher potential for outperformance.

SCHY's expense ratio was 0.08%. But over the past year, the latter has significantly outperformed SCHY, up close to 39% compared to roughly 21%. I would say that JIVE's actively managed portfolio had a lot to do with this.

Even when you compare JIVE's performance in the past year to the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO) and the tech-concentrated ETF, Invesco's QQ Trust ETF (QQQ), JIVE led in terms of price appreciation despite solid performances from both.

As stated previously, JIVE focuses on foreign, large-value stocks across all market caps. This is different than some ETFs, which typically focus on stocks with large market caps.

Instead, they focus on stocks trading at attractive valuations with strong fundamentals.

JIVE is a little over 2 years old, with a launch date of September 13th, 2023. And despite being relatively new, the ETF has grown its AUM to more than $1 billion at the time of writing. Their investment objective is to provide long-term capital appreciation.

In the chart below, you can see what $10,000 invested into the ETF at inception would be worth compared to QQQ and VOO.

$10k into JIVE since September of 2023 would have grown to $16,690, beating the tech-heavy QQQ and outpacing the largest S&P 500 (SP500) ETF, VOO.

Distributions & Taxes

The likely reason why QQQ slightly outperformed in dollar amount is that JIVE pays distributions on an annual basis versus quarterly like QQQ. Although, the latter does have a higher distribution yield in comparison at 1.72% vs 0.45% for QQQ.

While this is nothing to get overly excited about, this is higher than the S&P 500, which usually sits around 1% to 1.3% on average.

JIVE's distributions are considered ordinary income. So, they're better held inside a tax-advantaged account like a Roth IRA.

As a result, this could also make them more appealing for retirees looking for diversification into growth investments vs income-oriented ones.

Portfolio

Currently JPMorgan's International Value ETF's portfolio has 341 holdings. Their portfolio is heavily concentrated in the financial sector at nearly 36%. This may also be viewed as a risk. The other top two sectors that make up the portfolio are Industrials and Materials at 10.3% and 7.8%, respectively.

Below are the ETF's top 10 holdings. While some investors may be unfamiliar with JIVE's holdings, the portfolio includes notable, globally recognized companies like Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), Mercedes-Benz Group (MBGAF), British American Tobacco (BTI), Mitsubishi UFJ Financial Group (MUFG), and The Bank of Nova Scotia (BNS).

As far as geographic diversification, JIVE's portfolio is mostly concentrated in EMEA at 49.7%. They also have high exposure to Asia ex-Japan at 25.3%. Japan's exposure sits at 15.5%, while North America has 4.1% exposure, all concentrated in Canada.

NAV Growth

JIVE's strong performance so far can be attributed to its NAV growth. As you can see, their NAV growth has outpaced that of the index in the past 1 and 3 months and year-to-date.

Since inception, JIVE has beaten the index in NAV growth by roughly 6%, up 26.97%. For ETFs that see higher NAV growth, this is typically followed by strong price appreciation. The ETF was rated as one of the best non-U.S. ETFs last month, which also speaks volumes to its quality.

Risk To Thesis

One huge risk I believe JIVE could face is underperformance going forward. With the fund being relatively new and backed by powerhouse JPMorgan, this is likely a contributing factor to their strong growth.

But, like anything new, investors could lose interest, resulting in underperformance going forward. This is similar to Schwab's U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD).

It's also possible that U.S. stocks could continue to outperform international stocks over the long term, especially with the rapid adoption of AI. As a result, this could potentially lead to continued underperformance vs. U.S.-based equities.

Investor Takeaway

While JIVE is still relatively new, their outperformance vs. U.S.-based ETFs like VOO and QQQ over the past year is a testament to the ETF's underlying holdings.

Additionally, their actively managed strategy, reasonable expense ratio, and distribution yield make them attractive for investors looking for income and growth potential.

If you're currently looking for diversification outside of U.S.-based equities with a high likelihood to outperform in the coming years, then consider JPMorgan's International Value ETF for your portfolio.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Buy And Hold Portfolio For Next 10 Years: Potential $5,500 Monthly Income
data.description:
Introduction

The markets have been in a bull market since late 2022. The S&P 500 gained 26.1%, 24.8%, and 17.6% in 2023, 2024, and 2025 (until Nov. 30th), respectively. Also, we have seen the S&P500 make 35 new all-time highs in 2025 on top of 57 new all-time highs in 2024. However, the majority of gains have gone to a rather small number of companies, including the Mag-7 companies. In fact, Mag-7's market capitalization is now one-third of the market capitalization of all S&P 500 companies.

So what is driving those gains to be concentrated in a few companies? The answer is the AI revolution. Lately, any company doing something related to AI has been rewarded handsomely. The AI phenomenon is also driving raising and borrowing on an unprecedented scale. It is estimated that the AI infrastructure fund-raising and borrowings by hyper-scalers (like Alphabet, Oracle, Meta, and others), as well as power utilities, will exceed $5 trillion to build the data centers and other infrastructure for this new technology.

There is no doubt that the technology has a ton of potential and may even change the world in ways that are unthinkable today, but the question is whether the companies that are raising these huge amounts of money will be profitable enough to pay down those debts. This reminds us of the days of the internet (and dot-com) hype in the 90s. It does not mean the impacts of the internet were not real on our lives and the economy. In fact, they were real, but we still saw many of the companies at the peak of the hype could never recover. So, the possibilities of the "AI" revolution are endless, but the risks are real as well.

So, as such, this article is not about "AI" but about how to invest safely in this environment, where we do not take unnecessary risks, but are still able to benefit from the upside of the technology boom.

In this article, we will formulate a fund-based portfolio with three main objectives:

  1. Provide asset-based diversification so that we are not dependent on any one specific asset.
  2. Provide market-matching growth in terms of total returns. The portfolio should participate and benefit from the companies that are leading in science and technology.
  3. The portfolio should also provide a roughly 6% income yield, in case the investor needs to withdraw income.

Besides the above main objectives, the portfolio should be easy to implement and should not require very frequent monitoring.

Keeping all these goals in mind, we will structure a buy-and-hold fund-based portfolio that requires some serious planning in the beginning, but once implemented, it will mostly be a set-it-and-forget-it type.

Our goals require that we invest in a diversified set of asset classes. So, we may include the following asset types:

  • Large-cap blue-chip dividend stocks
  • High-growth Technology (and Innovative companies) stocks
  • Real estate and Preferred
  • Gold
  • Commodities and Materials
  • Energy
  • Utilities and Infrastructure companies

Our second goal is to get the market-matching total returns. So, we will ensure that we keep enough exposure to high-growth stocks to be able to meet this goal.

We also meet the income goal, so we will select ETFs and CEFs that pay sufficient income yield as well. However, at least one asset class, that is gold, pays no income yield.

Without much further ado, here are our selections for the portfolio. We will discuss the individual selections in the next section.

The Portfolio with diversified asset classes:

Table 1:

Note: The income column in the table above is calculated based on a $1 million total investment.

2) BST no longer writes call options.

The above portfolio will provide a pretty decent level of income, just over $65,000 a year, on a $1 million portfolio.

Sector Allocations:

We put the portfolio under Morningstar's X-ray tool (subscription required), and here are the results and comparison with the S&P500. We can see that this portfolio is much more defensive in nature and has only about 22% allocation to technology compared to 37% in the S&P500.

Chart-1A:

Return vs Risk Contribution (from 2015 to 2025):

Table-2C:

Note: GPIQ has only about a two-year-long history, so it was replaced with the Columbia & Seligman Premium Technology Growth Fund (STK).

Backtested Performance:

We compared the hypothetical performance of our model portfolio with the S&P 500 from Jan.1, 2015, until Nov.30, 2025; here is how it looks.

Also, below is the comparison of various metrics from the backtesting from 2015 to 2025. The total return performance is slightly lower than the S&P500, but that is due to the fact that over the last 10 years, the S&P500 has had exceptional performance due to the technology boom and its large concentration in the tech sector. However, the model portfolio had its worst year at -12% while the S&P500 was at -18%.

If we wanted to compare very long-term performance, we would have to replace several securities with other similar funds/securities. So, we replaced SCHD with ADX, BUI with UTG, AMLP with Enterprise Products Partners LP (EPD), and GPIQ and BST with Eaton Vance Enhanced Eqty Inc Fund II (EOS). With these replacements, we were able to backtest starting from 2006. Here is the comparison.

Chart-2B: Performance Comparison from Jan.1, 2006, until Sep. 2025

Here are some details and our thoughts on individual selections in the portfolio.

Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD):

If you are an income investor, this is one dividend fund that demands consideration. It focuses on large, blue-chip dividend-paying companies. The reasons for the fund's popularity among dividend investors include wide diversification, moderate income, and reasonably good dividend growth.

SCHD currently pays a 3.73% yield (30-day SEC yield being 3.79%). The 12-month trailing yield was 3.87%. The fund charges very low fees (only 0.06%). The fund aims to track the Dow Jones U.S. Dividend 100 Index as its benchmark.

The past dividend growth has been quite outstanding at 10.46% and 10.84% during the last 5 and 10 years, respectively. That kind of growth could turn the initial yield of 3.7% into over a 10% yield on a cost basis in 10 years.

In terms of total returns, during the last 10 years, SCHD has provided an annualized return of 11.40%, which is significantly lower than the 14.4% return from the S&P500. However, SCHD has a more defensive portfolio with less than 10% exposure to technology stocks, while the S&P500 has nearly 36%. That's why it should come as no surprise that SCHD may underperform the S&P500 somewhat during the bull periods.

The Top 10 holdings of SCHD are Merck (MRK), Amgen (AMGN), Cisco Systems (CSCO), AbbVie (ABBV), Bristol-Myers (BMY), Coca-Cola (KO), PepsiCo (PEP), ConocoPhillips (COP), Chevron (CVX), and Verizon (VZ).

Adams Diversified Equity Fund (ADX):

Like SCHD, ADX invests in the equity of large US companies and uses no leverage, but that is where the similarities end. ADX invests more in high-growth stocks, and they don't have to be dividend-paying stocks. The allocation to Technology stocks is at 35%, much similar to the S&P500. That's why ADX has not only met the performance of the S&P500 but exceeded it during the last 5 and 10 years. The fund's core philosophy is to invest in a broadly diversified, sector-neutral, large-cap equity portfolio.

The fund pays a managed distribution on a quarterly basis. The fund's current policy is to distribute 2% of average net asset value ("NAV") every quarter, making it roughly 8% of the NAV on an annual basis.

The fund is internally managed and charges much lower fees (at roughly 0.64%) than its peers. In terms of performance, this fund is no laggard. The fund has a very long and excellent performance history. In the past five and 10-year periods, it has returned 17.3% and 16.3% (14.7% and 14.1% on a NAV basis), respectively, on an annualized basis. Also, currently, the fund is trading at roughly a -6% discount, which has narrowed in recent times.

Some top holdings of the fund are similar to those of the S&P500, such as Nvidia (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), Alphabet (GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), Meta (META), Broadcom (AVGO), JPMorgan Chase (JPM), and Tesla (TSLA). Also, the allocation to the Technology sector is similar to the S&P500.

Cohen & Steers REIT & Preferred Inc Fund (RNP):

Real estate is an important asset class that should be represented in everyone's portfolio. We are selecting this hybrid fund, RNP, that represents real estate equity as well as preferred and bond securities.

The allocation between the real estate and preferred/bond securities is roughly 50%:50%, but it can vary a little bit at times. We are allocating 12% of the portfolio to RNP, and that would mean that we are allocating 7% to real estate. However, overall, we would have close to 10% allocated to real estate because other funds in the portfolio, like SCHD and ADX, also have real estate allocations inside those funds. We think an overall 10% allocation to real estate is appropriate.

The fund uses a significant amount of leverage, nearly 30%, which makes it more volatile and risky. If you do not like leverage, we would recommend using the Cohen & Steers Total Return fund (RFI), which is 80% allocated to real estate (and the balance to bonds/preferred) and does not use leverage. That said, the management team at RNP has done a reasonably good job of maintaining the NAV over the years while also providing high income.

As such, RNP is highly diversified with its over 300 holdings. On the real estate side, it mostly owns equity in well-known REITs (Real Estate Investment Trusts) and currently holds about 30 such names. It currently yields a respectable 8.1%. The fund trades at a small discount of -3.7%.

Over its 22-year history, it has compounded investors' capital at an annualized rate of 9.3% (as of Sep.30, 2025).

Goldman Sachs Nasdaq Premium Income ETF (GPIQ):

The ETF seeks high current income while providing equity exposure to high growth stocks in the Nasdaq-100 Index with potential for capital appreciation. It sells call options dynamically, which helps the fund to provide high income but at the same time provides potential outperformance in flat markets.

As the fund describes its options strategy, "To generate income, the Fund employs a dynamic options (overwrite) strategy whereby the Fund sells (writes) call options on a varying percentage of the market value of the equity investments in the Fund's portfolio (the "Option Strategy"). The Fund expects that, under normal circumstances, the overwrite level (i.e., the ratio of the notional value of call options sold by the Fund to the market value of the equity investments in the Fund's portfolio) will be between 25% and 75% of the value of the equity investments in the Fund's portfolio."

So, this dynamic nature of the overwrite strategy gives this fund an edge by overwriting less during the bull period but writing more during flat or volatile markets.

This ETF was launched only about 2 years back in October 2023, and that's a negative because the history is so short. However, during this period (since inception until Oct. 31, 2025), the fund has returned 33% compared to 27.7% for the Nasdaq-100. The 12-month trailing yield is very attractive at 9.41%.

BlackRock Science & Tech Trust (BST):

BST is a closed-end fund offered by the BlackRock group. It has a decent history of roughly 11 years.We are including both GPIQ and BST to have the benefit of diversification, even though the underlying goals are similar. However, BST no longer writes call options, instead aims for capital appreciation from its high growth investments.

Please see our recent review of this fund; the link is here.

SPDR Gold MiniShares ETF (GLDM):

This is a GOLD-based ETF that provides exposure to the important asset class of gold bullion (precious metals). The investment objective of GLDM is for its Shares to reflect the performance of the price of gold bullion, less its expenses. The assets of GLDM include only gold bullion, gold bullion receivables, and, at times, small amounts of cash, if any. We are choosing GLDM over SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD), as GLDM comes with much lower expenses/fees of only 0.10%. Since gold provided no ongoing income, the amount of fees/expenses charged by the fund becomes even more important.

Gold has its own cycles of bull periods followed by several years when it usually stays flat or trades sideways. We are currently witnessing a bull market. However, a small exposure to the gold asset is important to have as it protects the investor from inflationary cycles and long-term currency devaluation. It may be best to have physical gold, but that is not always possible due to a lot of other problems that come with it.

Vanguard Materials Index Fund ETF (VAW):

Usually, the broader indexes such as the S&P500 do not have sufficient allocation to the Materials sector. The sole purpose of this fund in our portfolio is to provide diversification and exposure to the commodities asset class, which tends to provide an inflation hedge (at least in the long run).

VAW is an exchange-traded fund offered by the Vanguard group that is focused on investments in the materials sector. The fees are pretty low at 0.10%, which is typical of most of Vanguard's index-based funds. The fund seeks to replicate the performance of its benchmark, the MSCI US Investable Market Materials 25/50 Index. The fund has a reasonable sum of assets under management of roughly $4 billion. Currently, it yields roughly 1.62%.

Even though the fund is structured on a market-cap-weighted basis, it offers a reasonable exposure to mid and small-cap stocks in the Materials sector. It currently has roughly 110 holdings, and the top 10 accounts for 57% of the assets. We will allocate 7% to this ETF.

As an alternative, one can consider the Materials Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLB), which should offer similar results. However, XLB is more concentrated, with only 29 holdings. XLB provides a slightly higher income yield at 1.95% compared to VAW.

Adams Natural Resources Fund (PEO):

Energy is an important asset class to have. It is usually more volatile, but in the long term, it pays reasonably well, and generally, the income yields are much higher than those of other stocks. One way off-course would be to invest in a few leading energy companies, but that may not work for everyone. How about if we delegate that responsibility to a well-managed fund? The fund not only provides high income yield, currently at 9% (paid quarterly on a managed basis), but also offers a large discount to NAV (currently at roughly -10%). The fund provided exposure to some of the largest and most well-recognized names in the Oil industry. The fund is a bit concentrated at the top. In fact, just two companies, Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Chevron (CVX), alone constitute about 37% of the assets.

Another positive is that the fund managers charge a much lower fee of 0.64% when compared to its peers. Some top holdings of the fund are well-known names such as Exxon Mobil (XOM), Chevron (CVX), ConocoPhillips (COP), Linde (LIN), Williams Cos (WMB), Phillips 66 (PSX), Marathon (MRO), and EOG Resources (EOG).

Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP):

The second fund that we will allocate to in the energy sector will be focused on Pipelines (transportation of energy).

The AMLP fund invests in energy companies and partnerships that are engaged in the transportation, storage, and processing of Oil, Gas, and other energy resources. The ETF is based on the underlying index, the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index (AMZI).

The Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index (AMZI) is a capitalization-weighted composite of energy infrastructure Master Limited Partnerships (MLPs) that earn the majority of their cash flow from midstream activities.

The fund has a relatively high fee/expenses of 0.85% and that is a big negative compared to owning the MLPs directly, but it simplifies the process of owning MLPs as the fund distributes the income as dividends rather than partnership income (or K-1 tax form). Nearly 54% of the assets are invested in cos/partnerships that are engaged in transportation (pipelines) of either Petroleum or Gas. Since it is capitalization-weighted, the largest 10 holdings constitute nearly 93% of the assets. In all, it has 13 holdings at this time. Its 12-month trailing dividend is 8.19%.

Reaves Utility Income Fund (UTG) and BlackRock Utility, Infra & Power Trust (BUI):

The "utilities and infrastructure" is an important investment class, especially for conservative income-focused investors. We are dividing our exposure to the two best-managed utility funds, UTG and BUI. More recently, the utility fund has had a nice run due to likely higher power demands from the AI and data-center investments. So, as a consequence, the yields are much lower compared to two years ago.

Both UTG and BUI are closed-end funds, with UTG having a longer history. These funds seek to invest in utility companies of all kinds that cater to different segments, such as electric, gas, water utilities, and communications. In addition, they have varying degrees of exposure to industrial and midstream energy companies as well.

One big difference between UTG and BUI is that UTG uses moderate leverage, or roughly 18% while BUI uses none.

The objective here is to get some exposure to the utility and infrastructure companies that are usually more conservative but provide good income. As such, they are not growth funds, but both funds have delivered decent returns in the past. For example, both UTG and BUI have returned close to 12% annualized returns in the last 10 years (assuming reinvested distributions).

Risks and potential concerns:

There are some risks that an investor should be aware of prior to investing in the strategy covered above.

  • Market risk: Barring a couple of investments, nearly all of the investments in this portfolio are invested in equity stocks, even if they belong to diverse assets. So, if the broader market were to correct or take a deep dive for any unforeseen reason, this portfolio would also lose value.
  • No downside protection: Most of the portfolio has no downside protection. The only protection it may offer is that some of the securities belong to unrelated asset classes and may offer divergence from the stock market. That said, in case of a deep correction, it will likely lose value as much as the S&P500.
  • High valuations: The broader stock market is trading at all-time highs. As they say, the rising tide lifts all boats; almost nothing appears to be cheap. So, many of the funds in this portfolio may be trading at peak (or near the peak) valuations, and caution is required.

Concluding Thoughts

We believe the model portfolio of 5 ETFs and 6 CEFs is a complete and balanced portfolio. It is also very easy to implement as a buy-and-hold solution for those who prefer a hands-off approach.

The portfolio is quite different than the S&P 500 and has much lower exposure to the technology sector, but it still matches the growth of the S&P 500 over the long term. In addition, it provides a 6.5% starting income yield (for income seekers) in addition to providing exposure to diverse asset classes. Moreover, it should require minimal maintenance, but a yearly review or balancing would be recommended.

Now, a word about the valuations. The broader market is at all-time highs currently, and that means not all funds suggested for the portfolio are cheap in terms of valuation. So, it may be best to accumulate these funds in multiple lots using the dollar-cost-average method.

data.title: Amrize: A Dual-Engine Compounder In The Making
data.description:
I am giving a hold rating to the Amrize AG (AMRZ) company, mainly because of its current valuation level that is not very attractive. Overall, Amrize is a good company, with well-diversified activities in the construction materials industry and with a strong operational resilience attested by the last quarter's results.

Its business model is working with the articulation of two different components: strong volumes of heavy materials and high-margin activities in the Building Envelope sector. This dual focus is good for stability, but with its current levels of P/E and EV/EBITDA, I think that the market has already appropriately priced the "soft landing" scenario of construction markets, and investing in the company now would imply that you have an even more optimistic view of the market: I don’t think that this is a very reasonable bet to take.

The underlying industry is cyclical, and while there are indeed reasons to be optimistic about the infrastructure market, given that the construction market is still soft, I would rather wait for an entry point with an EV/EBITDA below 10x (now 12.23x on a forward basis).

Growth Amidst Challenges

Following Q3 2025, Amrize proved that despite operational hurdles that can happen, the cost management remains very effective and the company is able to show favorable growth metrics.

Indeed, the top line reached $3.7 Bn (a 6.6% YoY increase), with $2.8 Bn coming from the high volume Building Materials activity and the remaining $0.9 Bn coming from the high-margin Building Envelope activity. Overall, the 6.6% YoY growth was mostly driven by both volume increases in the cement market (6%) and in the aggregates market (3.3%). Clearly, the activity is being driven up by infrastructure projects, mainly data centers, with a market projected to grow at a 13.4% CAGR until 2034.

The Building Envelope activity was clearly the key source of growth of the last quarter, and not only did the top line grow, but also its margin. The adjusted EBITDA margin reached 24.1%, a 1.90 percentage point increase. Of course, a reason behind this growth is the lower prices of raw materials, but this is also attributable to both the operational efficiency of the company and the fact that they won market share in the commercial roofing market, increasing pricing power.

At the same time, one reason to not be overly optimistic about the company is its vulnerability to operational risks. A good example is the recent $50 Mn hit to the adjusted EBITDA of the Building Materials activity because of an equipment outage in a cement plant that lasted 6 weeks. The loss was limited, and the company managed to be flexible and addressed the situation with other logistic solutions to supply customers with other plants, and the issue is now resolved, but I worry about the 6-week time frame that really doesn’t appear to be efficient and that could hide stronger risks in other plants of the company.

The detail above was, of course, not a severe drag if you’re thinking about the big picture, I just think it's a relevant point to have in mind. The cash flow generation increased 49% YoY even with that to reach $674 Mn, effectively used to reduce the leverage of the company by $612 Mn, and the leverage ratio is now below 1.7x: close to the 1.5x target for Q4.

The FY 2025 guidance was also adjusted with a stronger top line, now estimated to come between $11.7 Bn to $12.0 Bn, but the Adjusted EBITDA remains expected to be between $2.9 Bn to $3.1 Bn. This means that the demand is more dynamic than expected, consistent with strong data center trends unfolding, but the cost friction that occurred during Q3 is going to be an issue at the end of the year, as the management does not expect to make up for it in Q4. Is the current level of margins sustainable in the long term? I would assume a slightly lower level to avoid a negative surprise.

"Fair" is Not "Cheap"

Now that we have looked into the key evolutions of the company and the dynamics of these results, it is time to look at the valuation. You will find below a selection of peers in both markets addressed by the company (Heavy Building Materials and Building Envelope):

From there, I can point out that against pure players of the Heavy Building Materials industry such as Vulcan Materials (VMC) and Martin Marietta (MLM), Amrize is trading at a lower multiple. This actually makes sense from the trends we saw above, Heavy Building Materials companies are currently navigating a strong underlying market and are trading at higher multiples than other companies with a more diluted focus.

This leads us to look at the valuation of peers with more diversified activities, such as CRH plc (CRH) and Carlisle Companies (CSL), trading at slightly higher multiples than Amrize. I think that this slight discount is consistent with the recent cost issue during Q3 and the fact that the company has recently been spun-off. Overall, spun-off companies are underperforming, as you can see from the chart below comparing the performance of the S&P United States BMI index, and the S&P U.S. Spin-Off Index, a subsection of the US BMI index.

Compared to Eagle Materials (EXP) and Owens Corning (OC), which are operating in the less dynamic Building Envelope market, Amrize is trading at a premium, also explained by the larger size of the company and its leading position.

I see the current valuation of Amrize as fair, and unless there is a clear sign that the company is going to leverage significant synergies, or unlock new growth sources, I wouldn’t recommend buying with an EV/EBITDA multiple around 12.

Capitalizing on Megatrends

Despite the above, I do not think that there is going to be volatility in valuation multiples, because Amrize has a strong strategic positioning and a stable position backed by important long-term trends. In short, I don’t think that there is a downside risk, but not much of an upside potential either.

The first one is related to federal infrastructure spending and the need to modernize the current infrastructure. During the earnings call, the management highlighted that the priority of the government is now going to modernization projects, which is a strong tailwind for the company: it limits the downside potential because there will always be a floor in the demand for aggregate and cement, diminishing the effect from the volatility induced by residential markets.

The second one is the increase of industrial activities in the US, and onshoring. Mainly, the demand related to data centers is strong, with 25 projects being supplied by Amrize in 2025. We know that the demand for data centers is going to increase in the next few years (study referred to above), and this also contributes to the low probability of a contraction in the trading multiple of the company. In addition, the development of LNG activities in the US is benefiting the company; these are concrete-intensive projects driving the demand for Amrize products, and the fact that they are one of the few players able to cover needs for massive projects in a reliable way (for example, in Q3 there was a problem in one factory, and the supply chain of the company adapted to cover the needs, but this increased costs) is a competitive advantage.

Conclusion

Amrize is definitely a high quality company, with diversified and low risk operations thanks to strong underlying trends, but the risk/reward profile now appears to be very neutral. The recent results of the company highlighted were evident when looking at its positioning: resiliency and strong cash generation, but the current valuation is also, in my opinion, perfectly pricing the smooth soft landing scenario currently unfolding.

Owning Amrize in the long term is probably a good idea, and the current valuation is not too high. However, I would rather wait for it to become more attractive before buying, as there is only a very limited upside for now. Yes, there is a risk that the valuation never contracts, so if you are interested in Amrize, I would recommend starting with a small position for now, and increasing it if the valuation gets closer to a 10x EV/EBITDA multiple.

data.title: These 2 Are, By Far, My Favorite Mag-7 Stocks
data.description:
Introduction

If there's one thing I'm not known for, it's my investments in Big Tech. That's because there aren't any. While I am typing this, I own 16 stocks, none of which are part of the information technology sector. Zero.

The only Big Tech stock I have owned in recent years is Apple (AAPL), which I sold after less than a year, if my memory is correct. I'm sure most of my regular readers do not even know I owned it at all.

This has a number of reasons, one of which is that I never liked the competition between Big Tech stocks. Who knows what the next big thing is in tech? Having witnessed a person very close to me disrupt major companies through his own inventions, I wasn't eager to invest in companies that are one big idea away from being obsolete.

Obviously, I am painting with a very broad brush here. I'm not making the case that any of the Mag-7 are that fragile. In fact, I believe all have wide moats, with some moats being wider than others.

Another reason was that, since 2021, when I got serious about dividend investing (my career goes back much further), I have been more focused on value stocks, as I believe in "higher for longer" inflation and because we had two major drawdowns in cyclical value stocks in recent years (we're currently in one of these drawdowns).

In other words, I simply found other investments more compelling. And, since 2021, it has not kept me from beating the market (see below), so I'm not complaining.

That said, I have spent countless hours discussing the AI trend this year in my daily Seeking Alpha articles. We have discussed many angles, including power suppliers, new competition between the Mag-7, and the massive disruption.

In many of these articles, I promised I would present my two favorite Big Tech stocks that I expect to beat both the S&P 500 and their peers going forward.

So, let's get right to it!

It's All About New Competition & Disruption

There are a few important things to keep in mind. One of them is the fact that the biggest tech stocks dominate this market. This is also a very obvious fact, as I doubt anyone reading this is surprised by this statement.

Nobody reading this has ever experienced a market this top-heavy.

Moreover, the second important thing to mention is that these large tech companies are increasingly going after each other's market share.

  • Competition in cloud computing has heated up tremendously.
  • In current AI computing, it's all about having the best LLM model. Here, companies like Alphabet (GOOGL), xAI, OpenAI, Microsoft (MSFT), and Anthropic battle each other for the crown.
  • Even in personal electronics, it's hard to guess what the future will look like, as technology could forever change the way we use smartphones, PCs, and other gadgets.

Here's what that AI model leadership battle looked like this year:

Interestingly, this has now created a situation where investors have jumped into Apple (AAPL) to avoid some of these risks, as Bloomberg reported on December 9. After initially suffering from underperformance because Apple isn't one of the hyperscalers nor known for any big AI innovations, it has crushed (I think that's an appropriate word in this case) the market and the Mag-7 index. This includes NVIDIA (NVDA). I was surprised.

Basically, because Apple doesn't play a part in the CapEx-heavy AI battle, it's suddenly a favorite among investors:

“While they most certainly will incorporate more AI into the phones over time, Apple has avoided the AI arms race and the massive capex that accompanies it,” said Bill Stone, chief investment officer at Glenview Trust Company, who owns the stock and views it as “a bit of an anti-AI holding.” - Bloomberg

This isn't my way of saying that Apple is a buy, but one of my favorite examples of how AI and general market conditions have changed the influence of the Mag-7 group.

Now, let me show you my favorites, the two picks I expect to beat the Mag-7, S&P 500, and, for what it's worth, Apple (AAPL) as well.

#1: Alphabet (GOOGL) - I Loved It When It Was Cheap, And I Still Do

I have been a fan of Alphabet since it was "cheap" earlier this year. Back then, the market was worried Alphabet would be a victim of AI, as modern models may make Google Search a thing of the past. Well, bears were wrong, as the stock has made many all-time highs this year. Its 3-year performance is now roughly 240%. Unfortunately, while I was bullish, I didn't buy the stock.

In the second half of this year, I was dealing with my Albanian real estate investment and simply lacked the capital to buy any new stock.

However, that's OK, as I know that many readers have done really well owning the stock. I also believe this rally has legs, which is why Alphabet is one of my two favorite Big Tech stocks.

What I love about Alphabet is its innovative capabilities with support of its massive installed base of billions of active daily users across platforms like Gmail, YouTube, Maps, Google Search, and Gemini. Not only is Gemini now leading other LLM models in innovative power, according to major public betting markets like Kalshi, but it also competes with companies like Adobe (ADBE) because of how efficiently it allows people to change pictures. This is one of many benefits, including coding (as we can see below).

According to its 3Q25 call, Gemini has 650 million monthly active users. Total queries on this app have more than tripled between the second and third quarters. I know I say this a lot, but think about that for a second. Also, its cloud backlog soared by almost 50%, thanks to massive enterprise AI demand.

This is what their CEO, Sundar Pichai, said during the call:

We are bringing AI to more people and developers than anyone else. In July, we announced that we processed 980 trillion monthly tokens across all our surfaces. We are now processing over 1.3 quadrillion and monthly tokens, more than 20x growth in a year. Phenomenal. - GOOGL 3Q25 Earnings Call

Personally, I believe this is just the start, as Google is also leading in vision-language models, which I consider to be the future. So far, it has seen massive successes in Gemini, which also bodes well for its self-driving car segment, Waymo, which I think will be a major pillar of "tangible AI," so to speak.

Even better, Alphabet is producing super-efficient TPU chips, competing with NVIDIA and others. So far, with big success, according to CNBC:

Google’s TPUs are the leader in ASICs for AI, and some people think those chips are technically on par or even superior to Nvidia’s GPUs, “Chip War” author Chris Miller told CNBC. - CNBC

All of this is expensive, though. CapEx alone (excluding R&D) has risen by 83% in 3Q25. That's on top of 62% growth in 3Q25. Alphabet now spends $24 billion per quarter on CapEx.

The good news is that it's backed by impressive cash flows. As we can see below, net operating cash flow has risen by almost 60% to $48 billion. Free cash flow (that's operating cash flow after CapEx) grew by 39%.

In other words, if you allow me to paint with a broad brush again, Alphabet is accelerating spending, doing it very successfully, and even growing free cash flow. It has an AA+ credit rating.

Unfortunately, Alphabet isn't as cheap as I would like it to be. After failing to buy it when I started to like it, I have now watched it go up to 30x earnings. The good news is that this company is expected to grow its EPS by 32% this year, potentially followed by 5% and 15% growth in 2026 and 2027, respectively (see below).

As I believe these numbers could be beaten if it keeps leading in AI, with a lot more long-term potential, I stick to a Buy rating and believe this company is truly one of the best Big Tech stocks.

#2: Amazon.com (AMZN) - My Fingers Are Itching To Add It To My Portfolio

Amazon is one of the few companies with dot com in their name that turned out to be a good investment during the Dot-com bubble. As we all know, it has become the world's biggest e-commerce giant, and there are only a few select nations where it doesn't dominate. The Netherlands, where I currently live, is one example, as Bol.com is still the go-to place for most people.

And then there are nations like Albania, where there simply isn't a well-structured e-commerce/warehousing network to facilitate companies like Amazon. It's not a third-world country, it just has never been the focus of these giants.

Anyway, I'm not getting into e-commerce that much, as that's old news.

I thought a lot about how I would describe Amazon's story. And I think I found the answer. Basically, Amazon is in a transformation. It has gone from an e-commerce company that wants to grow at any cost to a giant with a much bigger focus on margins. After all, it's fair to say it has won the e-commerce battle. Now, it needs to make as much money as possible.

Again, I'm painting with that very broad brush of mine, but you get the point.

To me, this isn't about expanding the e-commerce empire as much as it's about margins. In a recent article, I included the chart below, which shows the S&P 500's biggest companies, including their profit margins.

Amazon is a clear outlier, as it has the lowest profit margins among the Mag-7. This is obviously due to its low-margin e-commerce business.

However, I think that's the biggest opportunity.

See, if we assume it will be hard to grow margins by a lot for the others, it means revenue growth will have to drive future capital gains. For Amazon, even small margin improvements could drive substantial gains.

That's the core of my thesis.

There are quite a few ways for Amazon to grow its margins. One of them is to keep using its Prime ecosystem to retain as many users as possible. If it maintains and even grows its e-commerce footprint, it can make way more money on third-party seller commissions, fulfillment fees, and related operations.

Then there's advertising. As many of you who are Prime Video members may have found out, advertising is getting more aggressive. The same goes for its e-commerce website. Amazon Ads generated roughly $18 billion in revenue in 3Q25 with a 22% growth rate.

It's a super impressive advertising business that uses AI for optimization and a ton of partnerships:

Last quarter, I mentioned our partnership with Roku, and we've built on that with a partnership with Netflix, providing advertisers using Amazon DSP with direct access to Netflix's premium ad inventory. We announced integrations with Spotify and SiriusXM. With Spotify, we provide advertisers with direct programmatic access to a global audience of more than 400 million monthly ad-supported listeners. And with SiriusXM, brands can reach 160 million monthly digital listeners across services like Pandora and SoundCloud, and we're excited about the advertising opportunity around Prime Video live sports. Live sports got a lot of interest from advertisers in upfront negotiations for 2025, '26, and we exceeded our own expectations for upfront commitments with significant growth across the board. - AMZN 3Q25 Earnings Call

And then there's Amazon Web Services, which made a huge comeback in 3Q25 with a 20.2% growth rate. This told investors it is still growing fast despite being the market leader (size tends to come with growth headwinds). AWS alone generates $33 billion in quarterly revenues and $11 billion in operating income.

During its call, Amazon explained that its AWS services have the best infrastructure, security, and capabilities to provide what businesses and governments are needing in terms of compute.

AWS has double the capacity it had in 2022. It will double again by 2027.

On top of that, it is developing its own chips. Amazon is still working with NVIDIA but increasingly doing in-house work to cut costs and sell to outside customers. I love the margin potential this has.

This capacity consists of power, data center, and chips, primarily our custom silicon, Trainium and NVIDIA. We've recently brought Project Rainier online, our massive AI compute cluster spanning multiple U.S. data centers and containing nearly 500,000 of our Trainium2 chips. Anthropic is using it now to build and deploy its industry-leading AI model, Claude, which we expect to be on more than 1 million Trainium2 chips by year-end. Trainium2 continues to see strong adoption, is fully subscribed, and is now a multibillion-dollar business that grew 150% quarter-over-quarter.

Today, Trainium is being used by a small number of very large customers, but we expect to accommodate more customers starting with Trainium3. We're building Bedrock to be the biggest inference engine in the world and in the long run, believe Bedrock could be as big a business for AWS as EC2, and the majority of token usage in Amazon Bedrock is already running on Trainium. - AMZN 3Q25 Earnings Call

And then there's e-commerce efficiency. Amazon currently has more than 1 million robots and expects to massively boost that number. Personally, I think Amazon will be one of the biggest mass-adopters of AI in robotics as soon as commercial availability becomes better (we're still in early stages). When adding that the company has such a dense warehousing network, I think it will be incredibly hard to disrupt the business.

Also, given that Amazon is focusing on even shorter delivery times and consumer staple goods, I think it could even add a Costco-like benefit where customers go for good deals and great prices for daily goods.

Here's a great quote from the Seeking Alpha news article:

"By introducing fresh groceries into our Same-Day Delivery service, we’re creating a quick and easy experience for customers. They can order milk alongside electronics; oranges, apples, and potatoes with a mystery novel; and frozen pizza at the same time as tools for their next home improvement project—and check out with one cart and have everything delivered to their doorstep within hours," stated the Seattle-based company. - Seeking Alpha

So, what does all of this mean for its valuation?

Currently, Amazon trades at 32x earnings. This year, EPS is expected to grow by 30%, potentially followed by 9% and 23% growth in the two years ahead.

Mathematically speaking, a 30x P/E ratio implies 12-13% annual capital gains.

I think it will be higher, as I believe we could see a huge surprise in operating efficiencies and potentially a much higher share of AWS and advertising in the revenue mix.

That's why I like it so much.

While it doesn't pay a dividend (yet), it would still make a lot of sense in my portfolio as a tech holding.

Takeaway

I'm not the biggest fan of Big Tech as an investment for the next decade.

However, there's still great value to be found.

As I explained in this article, I prefer Amazon and Alphabet, two giants that each have unique capabilities that could give them an edge in what seems to be increasing pressure on everyone's moat.

Alphabet has become an AI leader instead of a victim. It leads in LLM models, has next-gen chips, and a massive installed base that allows it to quickly turn ideas into cash flows.

Amazon is the clear margin play. It has the lowest margins and what seems to be the most fat to trip. On top of that, it also has success in chips, cloud leadership, and a double-A credit rating.

I love both and believe they have what it takes to beat the market for years to come.

Risks To My Thesis

The biggest risk is competition. As convinced as I am that these giants may thrive, it is very hard to estimate what the future holds, as AI innovation has gone into overdrive. Alphabet could suffer if competitors create better models, while Amazon could be disrupted if mass adoption of AI creates e-commerce headwinds. I think Amazon has lower disruption risks than Alphabet, yet, in general, it's the one big factor that could derail the bull case.

Another factor is regulation. Both companies have become so big that they are consistently being monitored. If they keep growing and expanding, forced breakups cannot be ruled out to lower monopoly risks. This, too, is very hard to forecast.

Up for a challenge? Test your knowledge on the biggest events in the investing world over the past week. Take the latest Seeking Alpha News Quiz and see how you stack up against the competition.


It was a contrasting week for Wall Street's major averages: the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500) and the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) posted losses, but the blue-chip Dow (DJI) gained. Sentiment was mixed: a less hawkish-than-expected Federal Reserve interest rate decision was offset by a slump in the artificial intelligence trade.

The central bank's updated projections showed no more rate cuts for this year or beyond. Traders felt encouraged by the Fed's economic growth and unemployment rate projections. This positive mood helped the S&P 500 (SP500) and the Dow (DJI) achieve new record closes on Thursday, the first time since late October and mid-November, respectively.
